36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.

41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.

96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.

162	- ...

167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations

85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. 126	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
127	
128	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
129	
130	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
131	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
132	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
133	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
134	
135	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. The body lifts
136	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
137	the small Zariski site of `X` (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
138	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
139	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
140	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
141	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
142	
143	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
144	
145	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
146	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
147	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
148	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
149	
150	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. The body inherits
151	the morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
152	sheafification (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
153	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
154	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
155	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
156	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
157	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
158	
159	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
160	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
161	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
162	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
163	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
164	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
165	law is definitional. -/
166	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
167	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
168	
169	/-- **The sheaf-level dual `M^∨ := ℋom_{𝒪_X}(M, 𝒪_X)`** of an `𝒪_X`-module.
170	
171	For a scheme `X` and `M : X.Modules`, the dual `dual M : X.Modules` is the
172	sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules dual `PresheafOfModules.dual` of the
173	underlying presheaf of `M` (the internal hom into the structure presheaf,
174	`M^∨(U) = ℋom_{𝒪_X|_U}(M|_U, 𝒪_X|_U)`).
175	
176	Construction = the **exact dual analogue of `tensorObj`** (this file, `tensorObj`):
177	apply the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 …)` on the
178	small Zariski site of `X` to the (axiom-clean, sub-step-3) presheaf dual
179	`PresheafOfModules.dual M.val`. The scheme's structure presheaf `X.presheaf` is
180	`CommRingCat`-valued over the single-universe topological site `Opens X`, hence is
181	exactly the base `R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}` that `PresheafOfModules.dual`
182	requires (the value `M^∨(U) = M|_U ⟶ R|_U` is an `R(U)`-module, needing
183	commutativity) — no CommRingCat/RingCat re-bridging is needed, since
184	`tensorObj` already takes `(R := X.presheaf)` over the same CommRingCat presheaf
185	and `X.ringCatSheaf.val = X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` definitionally.
186	
187	The sheafification functor already lands in `SheafOfModules`, so no manual
188	`Presheaf.IsSheaf` / sheaf-condition descent is needed (sheafifying an already-sheaf
189	gives an iso object; this is the file's convention, matching `tensorObj`).
190	
191	Per blueprint `lem:internal_hom_isSheaf` (§`sec:tensorobj_dual_infra`); Stacks
192	tags 01CM (internal hom into a sheaf is a sheaf) / 01CR item 2. This is the
193	`⊗`-inverse candidate of an invertible sheaf, feeding `exists_tensorObj_inverse`. -/
194	noncomputable def dual {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
195	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
196	      (PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := X.presheaf) M.val) :
197	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
198	
199	/-- **The sheaf-level dual is contravariantly functorial in isomorphisms.** An isomorphism
200	`e : M ≅ M'` in `X.Modules` induces `dual M' ≅ dual M`, obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level
201	dual iso `PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso` of the underlying presheaf isomorphism. This is the
202	reusable "dual respects isos" ingredient (the dual analogue of `tensorObjIsoOfIso`) feeding the
203	assembly of `dual_isLocallyTrivial`: a trivialisation `L.restrict f ≅ 𝒪` yields, contravariantly,
204	`dual 𝒪 ≅ dual (L.restrict f)`. -/
205	noncomputable def dualIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules} (e : M ≅ M') :
206	    dual M' ≅ dual M :=
207	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
208	    (PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso (R₀ := X.presheaf)
209	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e))
210	
211	/-! ## §2. (Off the critical path) the full monoidal-category structure
212	
213	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
214	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
215	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
216	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal
217	coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and no `MonoidalClosed` structure is ever
218	consumed. The earlier `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance (and the two
219	localization-transport supplements `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
220	`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) routed through the verified-absent
221	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall; they are removed here. The group
222	law is built directly on the line-bundle subcategory from the four
223	existence-of-iso lemmas below, mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`. -/

225	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
226	
227	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
228	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
229	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
230	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
231	
232	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
233	
234	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
235	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
236	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
237	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
238	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
239	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
240	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
241	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
242	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
243	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
244	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
245	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
246	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
247	
248	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
249	sheaf.**
250	
251	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
252	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
253	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
254	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
255	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
256	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
257	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
258	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
259	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
260	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
261	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
262	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
263	
264	/-- **Left unitor for `⊗_X`.** `𝒪_X ⊗_X M ≅ M`. (Blueprint
265	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, left half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor`.)
266	Sheafification of the presheaf-level left unitor `λ_ M.val`, composed with the
267	sheafification counit identifying `sheafification M.val` with the (already-sheaf)
268	`M`. The cheap `mapIso` pattern; uses no abstract pullback. -/
269	noncomputable def tensorObj_left_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
270	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) M ≅ M :=
271	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
272	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).leftUnitor M.val) ≪≫
273	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
274	
275	/-- **Right unitor for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X 𝒪_X ≅ M`. (Blueprint
276	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, right half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor`.)
277	Sheafification of the presheaf-level right unitor `ρ_ M.val`, composed with the
278	sheafification counit. -/
279	noncomputable def tensorObj_right_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
280	    tensorObj M (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≅ M :=
281	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
282	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).rightUnitor M.val) ≪≫
283	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
284	
285	/-- **Braiding for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X N ≅ N ⊗_X M`. (Blueprint
286	`lem:tensorobj_comm_iso`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding`.)
287	The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category is symmetric; its braiding `β_ M.val
288	N.val` sheafifies to the asserted isomorphism by the cheap `mapIso` pattern. -/
289	noncomputable def tensorObj_braiding {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) :
290	    tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj N M :=
291	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
292	    (BraidedCategory.braiding
293	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) M.val N.val)
294	
295	/-- **Associator for `⊗_X`.** (Blueprint
296	`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso`.)
297	For arbitrary `M, N, P : X.Modules` there is an isomorphism
298	`(M ⊗_X N) ⊗_X P ≅ M ⊗_X (N ⊗_X P)`. This is the objectwise existence-of-iso datum the
299	group law consumes (associativity as a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`).
300	
301	**UNCONDITIONAL and axiom-clean (iter-238 ROUTE (d)).** No flatness or local-triviality
302	hypothesis is used: the earlier flatness route (`W_whisker{Right,Left}_of_flat`, needing
303	sectionwise flatness — false for a general line bundle on a non-affine open) is RETIRED.
304	Writing `a = PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)` and `η` the
305	sheafification-adjunction unit, the three-step composite is:
306	  1. `a(η_{M.val ⊗ᵖ N.val} ▷ P.val)` is iso, giving
307	     `(M ⊗ N) ⊗ P ≅ a((M.val⊗N.val) ⊗ P.val)`;
308	  2. `a.mapIso α : a((M.val⊗N.val)⊗P.val) ≅ a(M.val⊗(N.val⊗P.val))`, `α` the

694	/-! ## §5. The invertibility-carrier Picard group `picCommGroup`
695	
696	This is the by-hand commutative-group law on isomorphism classes of `⊗`-invertible
697	`𝒪_X`-modules (blueprint §`sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier`). Every group axiom is a single
698	existence-of-isomorphism `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` read as an equality of iso-classes; no
699	pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence and no `MonoidalCategory` instance is invoked.
700	The inverse is carried by the membership witness of `IsInvertible` itself. -/
701	
702	/-- **Step 1 — associator on `⊗`-invertible objects** (blueprint
703	`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible`). An immediate specialisation of the now
704	*unconditional* `tensorObj_assoc_iso`; the invertibility hypotheses are not consumed
705	(they match the blueprint statement). -/
706	noncomputable def tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N P : X.Modules}
707	    (_hM : IsInvertible M) (_hN : IsInvertible N) (_hP : IsInvertible P) :
708	    tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P ≅ tensorObj M (tensorObj N P) :=
709	  tensorObj_assoc_iso
710	
711	/-- **Middle-four interchange for `⊗_X`** (helper). For arbitrary `𝒪_X`-modules
712	`A, B, C, D`, there is an isomorphism `(A ⊗ B) ⊗ (C ⊗ D) ≅ (A ⊗ C) ⊗ (B ⊗ D)`,
713	assembled from the unconditional associator and the braiding (no coherence consumed).
714	Used to reassociate the four factors in `IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
715	private noncomputable def tensorObj_middleFour {X : Scheme.{u}} (A B C D : X.Modules) :
716	    tensorObj (tensorObj A B) (tensorObj C D)
717	      ≅ tensorObj (tensorObj A C) (tensorObj B D) :=
718	  tensorObj_assoc_iso ≪≫
719	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl A)
720	      (tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
721	        tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding B C) (Iso.refl D) ≪≫
722	        tensorObj_assoc_iso) ≪≫
723	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm
724	
725	/-- **Step 3 — `⊗`-invertibility is closed under tensor product** (blueprint
726	`lem:isinvertible_tensor`). If `M, M'` are `⊗`-invertible with inverses `N, N'`,
727	then `N ⊗ N'` is a tensor inverse of `M ⊗ M'`. -/
728	theorem IsInvertible.tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules}
729	    (hM : IsInvertible M) (hM' : IsInvertible M') :
730	    IsInvertible (Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M M') := by
731	  obtain ⟨N, ⟨e⟩⟩ := hM
732	  obtain ⟨N', ⟨e'⟩⟩ := hM'
733	  exact ⟨Scheme.Modules.tensorObj N N',
734	    ⟨tensorObj_middleFour M M' N N' ≪≫ tensorObjIsoOfIso e e' ≪≫ tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩
735	
736	/-- **Step 4 — the structure sheaf is `⊗`-invertible** (blueprint
737	`lem:isinvertible_unit`). Witness `𝒪_X`, iso `tensorObj_unit_iso`. -/
738	theorem isInvertible_unit {X : Scheme.{u}} :
739	    IsInvertible (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :=
740	  ⟨SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf, ⟨tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩
741	
742	/-- **Step 5 — the tensor inverse is determined up to isomorphism** (blueprint
743	`lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef`). If `M ⊗ N ≅ 𝒪_X` and `M ⊗ N' ≅ 𝒪_X` then
744	`N ≅ N'`, via the inverse-of-inverse chain. -/
745	theorem IsInvertible.inverse_unique {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N N' : X.Modules}
746	    (e : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
747	    (e' : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N' ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :
748	    Nonempty (N ≅ N') :=
749	  ⟨(tensorObj_right_unitor N).symm ≪≫
750	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl N) e'.symm ≪≫
751	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
752	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding N M ≪≫ e) (Iso.refl N') ≪≫
753	    tensorObj_left_unitor N'⟩
754	
755	/-- The setoid of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules: `M ∼ M'` iff there exists an
756	isomorphism `M ≅ M'` (blueprint `def:pic_carrier`). -/
757	instance picSetoid (X : Scheme.{u}) : Setoid {M : X.Modules // IsInvertible M} where
758	  r M M' := Nonempty ((M : X.Modules) ≅ (M' : X.Modules))
759	  iseqv :=
760	    ⟨fun _ => ⟨Iso.refl _⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ => ⟨e.symm⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ ⟨f⟩ => ⟨e ≪≫ f⟩⟩
761	
762	/-- **Step 2 — the invertibility-carrier Picard group** (blueprint
763	`def:pic_carrier`): the quotient of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules by isomorphism. -/
764	def PicGroup (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ := Quotient (picSetoid X)
765	
766	/-- Multiplication on `PicGroup X`: `[M] · [M'] := [M ⊗_X M']`, well-defined on
767	iso-classes by bifunctoriality (`tensorObjIsoOfIso`), landing in `PicGroup` by
768	`IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
769	noncomputable def picMul {X : Scheme.{u}} : PicGroup X → PicGroup X → PicGroup X :=
770	  Quotient.lift₂
771	    (fun a b => Quotient.mk _ ⟨tensorObj a.1 b.1, a.2.tensorObj b.2⟩)
772	    (by
773	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨b, hb⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨b', hb'⟩ ⟨ea⟩ ⟨eb⟩
774	      exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObjIsoOfIso ea eb⟩)
775	
776	/-- The inverse class on `PicGroup X`: `[M] ↦ [N]` for the membership witness `N`
777	of `IsInvertible M`, well-defined by `IsInvertible.inverse_unique`. -/
778	noncomputable def picInv {X : Scheme.{u}} : PicGroup X → PicGroup X :=
779	  Quotient.lift
780	    (fun a => Quotient.mk _
781	      ⟨Classical.choose a.2,
782	        a.1, ⟨tensorObj_braiding _ a.1 ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec a.2).some⟩⟩)
783	    (by
784	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨ea⟩
785	      refine Quotient.sound ?_
786	      -- both `Classical.choose ha` and `Classical.choose ha'` are tensor inverses of `a`
787	      have h1 : tensorObj a (Classical.choose ha) ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
788	        (Classical.choose_spec ha).some
789	      have h2 : tensorObj a (Classical.choose ha') ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
790	        tensorObjIsoOfIso ea (Iso.refl _) ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec ha').some
791	      exact IsInvertible.inverse_unique h1 h2)
792	
793	/-- **Step 6 — the invertibility-carrier Picard group is abelian** (blueprint
794	`thm:pic_commgroup`). `[M] · [M'] := [M ⊗_X M']`, `1 := [𝒪_X]`, and `[M]⁻¹` the
795	class of any membership witness of `IsInvertible M`. Each group axiom is a single
796	existence-of-isomorphism: unit laws ← unitors, associativity ← associator,
797	commutativity ← braiding, left inverse ← the witness iso. No monoidal coherence. -/
798	noncomputable instance picCommGroup (X : Scheme.{u}) : CommGroup (PicGroup X) where
799	  mul := picMul
800	  one := Quotient.mk _ ⟨SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf, isInvertible_unit⟩
801	  inv := picInv
802	  mul_assoc := by
803	    rintro a b c
804	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
805	    induction b using Quotient.ind with | _ b => ?_
806	    induction c using Quotient.ind with | _ c => ?_
807	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_assoc_iso⟩
808	  one_mul := by
809	    rintro a
810	    induction a using Quotient.ind with | _ a => ?_
811	    exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObj_left_unitor a.1⟩
812	  mul_one := by
813	    rintro a

225	/-! ## §1. Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient
226	
227	The Picard group `Pic(X)` of any scheme is canonically an abelian group
228	under tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of
229	`[L]` is `[L⁻¹] = [Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)]`). The pullback map
230	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_k T)` of
231	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection` (A.1.b)
232	respects this structure: it sends `O_T ↦ O_{C ×_k T}` (the structure
233	sheaf is preserved by inverse image) and is multiplicative on tensor
234	products (Stacks 01HH for invertibility, Mathlib's
235	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` for the underlying multiplicativity).
236	
237	Therefore `π_T^* Pic(T)` is a subgroup of `Pic(C ×_k T)`, and the
238	quotient `Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` inherits a canonical
239	abelian-group structure under which the quotient map is a surjective
240	homomorphism with kernel exactly `π_T^* Pic(T)`.
241	
242	Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Kleiman §2,
243	Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01CR). -/

456	/-! ## §2. The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued functor
457	
458	We assemble the data of A.1.b (object-level quotient set,
459	`RelPicPresheaf.functorial` morphism action) and §1 (abelian-group
460	instance on each quotient) into a single contravariant functor
461	
462	```
463	PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
464	```
465	
466	sending an `Spec k`-scheme `T` to the abelian group
467	`Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (with the structure of §1), and a
468	morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k` to the group homomorphism
469	`g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/k}(T')` descended from the
470	line-bundle pullback `g_C^* := (id_C ×_k g)^*`.
471	
472	Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_sharp` (Kleiman §2, Def. `df:Pfs`). -/

474	/-- The **relative Picard functor** of a smooth proper geometrically
475	integral curve `C` over a field `k`, as a contravariant functor
476	
477	```
478	PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
479	```

481	On objects: `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, the abelian-group
482	quotient of `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Lean instance
483	`PicSharp.addCommGroup`).
484	
485	On morphisms: `g ↦ g^♯`, the group homomorphism descended from
486	`g_C^* = (id_C ×_k g)^*` via the quotient (the set-level map is
487	`RelPicPresheaf.functorial`; the group-hom upgrade is
488	`PicSharp.functorial` below).

491	underlying carrier `Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` lives in

525	/-! ## §3. Functoriality (group-homomorphism strengthening)
526	
527	The naturality lemma `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b produces, for
528	each morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, a set map
529	```
530	g^♯ : Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T) ⟶ Pic(C ×_S T') / π_{T'}^* Pic(T')
531	```

533	both sides, this set map upgrades to an additive-monoid homomorphism
534	`AddMonoidHom`: indeed `g_C^*` preserves tensor products and the
535	structure sheaf, so it preserves the abelian-group operations on both
536	sides; the upgrade is the substantive content of
537	`lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial`.
